* The painter required 48 tiles for the fresco, and each tile needed 4 colors, so the total number of color applications was 48 multiplied by 4, resulting in 192 color uses.  
* Each color bottle could cover 10 tiles, meaning the painter needed 192 divided by 10, which equals 19.2 bottles of each color. Since partial bottles are not practical, the painter would need 20 bottles of each color to ensure there was enough paint for the entire project.  

The painter's task involved a significant amount of preparation and careful planning. The process of applying each color required precision and attention to detail. The painter had to ensure that each tile was painted correctly and that the colors blended seamlessly.  

The painter’s work was not only an artistic endeavor but also a technical one. He had to consider the properties of the pigments and how they would interact with the surface of the wall. Each color had to be applied in the right order and with the right amount of pressure to avoid smudging or fading.  

The painter’s dedication to his craft was evident in the care he took with every detail. He spent hours working on the fresco, ensuring that each tile was perfectly aligned and that the colors were applied with consistency. His work was a testament to the skill and discipline of Renaissance artists.  

The painter’s work was a reflection of the Renaissance spirit, which valued both beauty and knowledge. His fresco was not just an artistic creation but also a symbol of the era’s intellectual and cultural achievements. The painter’s attention to detail and his mastery of color made his work a masterpiece.  

The painter’s work was a masterpiece that would stand the test of time. His dedication to his craft and his ability to create something beautiful from the simplest of materials was a testament to the power of human creativity. The fresco would be remembered for generations to come.
